title: Nuno felting on cotton muslin
subtitle: Wool roving felted into cotton muslin fabric using the migration and agitation method
excerpt: Nuno felting bonds wool roving into an open-weave fabric, creating a pleated, textured surface as the wool migrates through the weave and contracts. Cotton muslin is a lower-cost base fabric than silk; migration takes longer and requires hotter water and more agitation, but the finished hand and pleat character are distinctive. The pinch test confirms when migration is complete.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the nuno felting tradition. The term 'nuno felting' was coined in the late 20th century to describe the process of felting wool into a woven fabric base. The cotton muslin variant is a lower-cost adaptation of the silk-scarf method; the migration behaviour on cotton differs from silk and is documented through guild practice rather than named printed sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method in which wool roving is laid onto an open-weave fabric and worked with wet heat and agitation until the wool fibres migrate through the weave structure and lock, bonding the wool permanently to the fabric. The fabric shrinks and gathers into a pleated or puckered surface texture as the wool contracts.
Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for felting. The fibres lie roughly parallel, making it easy to pull thin layers from the end and lay them onto a fabric surface.

The vigorous agitation stage that follows initial migration in nuno felting. Rolling the bundle tightly and working it with pressure compacts the bonded wool and drives further shrinkage, producing the characteristic pleated surface.

The stage at which wool fibres have begun to lock through the weave but migration is not yet complete. At pre-felt stage the wool can still be nudged into a new position; once migration is fully complete the wool cannot be repositioned.

content.text: In
nuno felting
, wool
roving

is laid onto the surface of an open-weave fabric and worked with hot soapy water until the wool fibres migrate through the weave structure and lock permanently into the fabric. The wool then contracts during
fulling
, gathering the fabric into pleats and creating a distinctive textured surface. The process works best on open-weave fabrics where the weave structure gives the wool fibres a path to pass through.

Silk is the traditional nuno base because its smooth fibres let the wool migrate quickly. Cotton muslin costs less and works differently. The cotton threads are slightly rougher than silk, which helps the wool grip, but migration takes longer because the cotton threads resist being pushed apart. On muslin, expect the migration phase to take 35 to 50 minutes. On fine silk the same stage takes 20 to 30 minutes. The finished bond is denser and the pleats are deeper and more pronounced than on silk.
What you need

items.name: Cotton muslin fabric, open-weave (not closely woven)
items.qty: 60 x 90 cm (pre-washed and dried without fabric softener)

items.name: Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (65 to 70°C — use a kettle and cool slightly)
Felting net or fine mesh (essential — holds roving in position)

content.text: Use a muslin with a visible open weave; finely woven cotton or polycotton blends do not allow migration and will not nuno felt. Pre-wash the muslin without fabric softener, as softener coats the fibres and impedes bonding. Fine merino migrates more readily than coarser breeds; 17 to 21 microns gives the best result on cotton. Coarser wool may not migrate fully and will sit on the surface rather than locking through.
Method
Step 1: Prepare the fabric and roving layout

Lay the cotton muslin flat on the bamboo rolling mat. Pull thin wisps of merino roving and lay them across the muslin surface in the pattern you want. For a uniform texture all over, lay a single thin layer of roving covering the fabric evenly, with all fibres running in the same direction. For a striped or patchy texture, lay roving in bands or clusters with bare areas in between. Leave 3 to 5 cm of the fabric edges bare of roving; these edges will pull in during shrinkage and any roving placed at the very edge may migrate off the fabric entirely.

The amount of roving determines the density of the finished piece. 40 g over 60 by 90 cm gives a light, airy nuno with visible fabric between the wool areas. 60 g gives a fuller, denser surface. Start with 40 to 50 g until you understand how the muslin behaves with this roving weight.
Step 2: Wet down through the felting net

Lay the felting net carefully over the entire roving surface. The net holds the roving in position while you wet it down; without it, pouring water directly displaces the wisps. Pour or spray hot soapy water at 65 to 70°C through the net to saturate both the roving and the muslin below. Work from the centre outward. The combination of heat and soap is essential for initiating migration on cotton muslin; lukewarm water will not produce sufficient migration within a working session.
Step 3: Begin rubbing through the net

With the net in place, begin rubbing the surface firmly with flat hands using circular motions. Apply consistent pressure across the full surface, not just the centre. Work for 5 minutes, then add more hot soapy water to keep the surface at temperature. Continue rubbing for a further 5 minutes. The total time through the net before the first pinch test is 10 minutes.
Step 4: The pinch test

Lift the felting net carefully from one corner and gently pinch a small tuft of wool between thumb and forefinger. Try to lift the wool away from the fabric surface. If the wool lifts cleanly away from the fabric without resistance, migration has not yet begun; replace the net and continue rubbing for another 5 to 10 minutes before testing again. If the wool resists the pinch and you feel a distinct pull on the fabric below, migration has started. If the wool cannot be lifted at all and the fabric gathers beneath the pinch point, migration is well underway or complete.

On cotton muslin, the first signs of migration typically appear after 25 to 35 minutes of working. Complete migration, where the pinch test shows no lift at all, takes 35 to 50 minutes total. Do not rush this phase; attempting to roll and full the piece before migration is complete will shift the roving across the fabric surface rather than locking it in.
Step 5: Remove the net and work the surface directly
Once the pinch test confirms migration is underway, remove the felting net. Continue rubbing the surface directly with hot soapy hands for a further 5 minutes. At this stage the
pre-felt
bond is strong enough that direct rubbing will not displace the roving. The surface will feel slightly gritty where the wool is beginning to grab the cotton threads.
Step 6: Roll and full to finish

Lay the fabric face-down on the bamboo mat. Roll the mat up tightly around the fabric to form a firm sausage. Roll the bundle back and forth on the worktop with firm hand pressure, pressing evenly along the full length, for 3 to 4 minutes. Unroll, unfold the fabric, turn it 90 degrees, and roll again. This rotation is essential for even shrinkage in all directions. The fabric will begin to gather and pleat visibly from the first rolling session.

Continue rolling in alternating directions. Add fresh hot water when the bundle cools. After every second or third rolling session, throw the bundle firmly onto the worktop surface 10 to 15 times to add impact agitation. Cotton muslin shrinks by 30 to 40 per cent of its original size as the wool contracts. The 60 by 90 cm starting fabric finishes at roughly 40 by 55 cm. Full until the wool surface feels dense and the fabric pleats are stable and cannot be stretched back flat.
Step 7: Rinse and dry

Rinse the piece thoroughly in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge into cold water for 30 to 60 seconds. The cold shock closes the wool fibre scales and firms the surface. Squeeze gently; do not wring. Lay flat on a dry towel and shape by hand to your preferred proportions. The pleats and gathers are stable and will dry into their current configuration. If you want flatter areas, press those areas by hand while the piece is still wet. Dry flat away from direct heat.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The roving shifted position during the early rubbing stage
items.cause: The felting net was removed too early, or the initial wetting disturbed the roving layout before the net was placed
items.fix: Keep the net in place until the pinch test confirms migration is underway. Wet the surface gently from above the net rather than pouring directly, and work with flat hands rather than directional strokes.

items.problem: The pinch test shows no migration after 40 minutes
items.cause: The water was not hot enough, the wool is too coarse to migrate through cotton, or the muslin weave is too tight
items.fix: Check the roving micron count: above 23 microns, migration through cotton is unreliable. Check the water temperature with a thermometer; it should be 65 to 70°C at the point of contact. Check the muslin: hold it up to a light and count the threads per centimetre. Above 20 threads per cm, the weave is too tight for nuno felting.

items.problem: The wool felted to itself on the surface but did not bond to the fabric
items.cause: Migration was incomplete when rolling began; the pressure consolidated the wool before it locked through the weave
items.fix: Test for migration before rolling. If the wool has already consolidated into a surface layer, re-wet, place the net back, and work the surface vigorously for another 15 minutes, then test again. In some cases the bond can still be driven through at this stage.

items.problem: The shrinkage is very uneven across the piece
items.cause: The roving was laid unevenly, with thick patches in some areas and bare areas in others
items.fix: Areas with more wool shrink more than lightly covered areas, which is normal and can be used deliberately for design effect. For even shrinkage, lay the roving in a uniform thin layer before wetting. Rotate the rolling direction regularly to prevent directional bias.

content.text: Care and finishing

Hand-wash in cool water with a wool wash, pressing and squeezing rather than rubbing. The wool-cotton bond is permanent and will not separate in gentle washing, but vigorous agitation will continue to felt the wool surface further and shrink the piece. Machine washing will cause further compaction and unpredictable distortion; avoid it. Dry flat.

Cotton muslin nuno can be cut and hemmed after felting. Because the wool locks through the cotton, raw edges do not fray in the same way as unworked cotton; a cut edge may be left unfinished for a deliberately raw look, or turned under and stitched with a blunt darning needle and matching thread. Press lightly with a steam iron on the reverse side at a medium cotton setting if you want to flatten the pleats partially; pressing on the wool face will mat the surface.
